title: Emacs is a Lispboard | Andros Fenollosa
excerpt: What would have happened if Emacs' default buffer had been a terminal emulator? Conceptually, from the outside, it would be perceived as a terminal on
content_html:
What would have happened if Emacs' default buffer had been a terminal emulator? Conceptually, from the outside, it would be perceived as a terminal on steroids with Lisp as its scripting language. Maybe we would be talking about iTerm2 vs Emacs, or Kitty vs Emacs, or even Bash vs Emacs. And what if the default buffer had been Dired, the file manager? History might have created rivalries such as Total Commander vs Emacs, or Finder vs Emacs.

But history took another path. We already know that if you launch an Emacs instance without any configuration whatsoever, what you see is a buffer with the following message:

;; This buffer is for text that is not saved, and for Lisp evaluation.
;; To create a file, visit it with ‘C-x C-f’ and enter text in its buffer.

A notepad with an embedded Lisp interpreter. Its natural rival is Vim with an embedded interpreter (Vimscript). However, the editor that made us all memorize :q! stayed in the buffer, while Emacs kept expanding with more and more software until it became an interface to the operating system.

I'm an Emacs enthusiast, and maybe that costs this article some credibility. However, I'm not here to tell you how fantastic the GNU editor is, nor to convince you to use it. My goal is to explain why the web browser is its true natural rival, and why Emacs is a Lispboard where you build the way you want to work.

Let's imagine a perfect working environment

To set the stage, let's do a little thought experiment.

Suppose I want to work in a single place for all my contexts, both personal and professional. That way I memorize my shortcuts, apply my configurations and never have to switch contexts. Everything lives under the same window.

It's a problem, because it means merging programs that have nothing to do with each other but need to collaborate. For example, a terminal emulator, an email client and a code editor (or IDE). With a layout system, inside the same window, I could have one column with my email inbox, another with the terminal and a third with my IDE ready to write whatever I need. In this space, it would be easy to write a bash script, launch it from the terminal and receive an email with the result. All in the same window, without switching context, with the same shortcuts, the same typography and the same theme. I could even add a fourth column with a Git client to version the script and push it to a remote repository. Or while we're at it, a fifth column with my favorite AI agent, able to read both my code and the rest of the panels.

We have just created an interface.

But this wouldn't be the most useful part: the programs would be integrated with each other, each one living in its own scope and, at the same time, in a global one. That means that if I enable the spell checker in the code editor with a certain configuration, it also becomes active in the terminal, in the email client when composing messages, or when I write commits. All with the same interactivity and configuration. They are not four isolated binaries connected with pipes: they are programs written in the same language, running on the same runtime and sharing the same event bus.

We have just created a connector.

Now let's remove an unnecessary layer: the bash script. If I already have an interpreter embedded in this very window, I can create my own scripts. I don't need an external binary. I no longer need to leave the window, nor touch the operating system. All my scripts, prototypes and workflows can live together.

We have just created a habitat.

There's only one problem left with the runtime: tomorrow I'll change laptops, or I'll want to work from a server, and I have no intention of rebuilding my environment from scratch. I want my scripts to work exactly the same regardless of the operating system and the hardware. A script should behave the same way on Windows, on a Raspberry Pi or on a cheap Android device. I need the whole environment to be a cross-platform runtime with bytecode that runs anywhere.

We have just created a virtual machine.

Now, to round off the idea, we'll use Lisp as the scripting language and we'll call the whole thing Emacs. Although I prefer to call it something else: a Lispboard. Like an electronics breadboard, a board where every module you plug in shares a bus with all the others, except here the modules are written in Lisp.

And if during this exercise you thought "this sounds familiar: tabs, applications and a cross-platform virtual machine... that's my browser", hold on to that intuition. We'll come back to it at the end of the article.

What is Emacs not?

With everything mentioned so far, I want to debunk some myths surrounding Emacs:

  • It's not a text editor: it's a complete working environment, with an embedded scripting language, its own software and a cross-platform runtime. The editor is just one more program inside that environment, as are the terminal, the email client, IRC, the file manager or the web browser (yes, Emacs ships with one).
  • It's not an operating system: it's a working environment that runs on top of an operating system, and that can replace many of the programs it offers. But it has no drivers, no kernel, no low-level tools. It cannot run without an operating system.
  • It's not an IDE: there is no default set of tools focused on programming in a specific language. For that you need LSP backends, debuggers and linters, which are installed as packages. But you can certainly get there; it's one of its most common uses.

What it is: a Lisp interpreter and a framework that run on top of the operating system in the most agnostic way possible, and that reimplement a good part of the programs it offers.

My workflow

Scripting and prototyping

When you think: "I'm going to write a simple bash script to automate a task", I think: "I'm going to write an Elisp function". And on top of that, I'll give it a graphical interface if I need flags or text input.

For example, suppose you need to create a script for yourself, not for a CI/CD process or cron. The goal is to back up a specific folder (maybe to the cloud). You could end up with a command like this:

./backup.sh /home/user/folder /home/user/backup

But you also include a flag to create a zip:

./backup.sh -z /home/user/folder /home/user/backup

Well, instead of going through the terminal, I create an Elisp function that does the same thing, and I add a small panel to it.

I run:

M-x my/backup RET

And a buffer will show up with an interface like this:

Folder backup

Folder: [ /home/user/folder ]
Target: [ /home/user/backup ]
[ ] Compress into zip

[ Cancel ] [ Run ]

With inputs, checkboxes and buttons. Without having to remember where it was, or its parameters, or their order.

If you have worked with Lisp before, you'll see the code is very basic:

(require 'widget)

;; Logic

(defun my/backup-run (folder target zip)
  "Sync FOLDER into TARGET with rsync, or compress it to a zip when ZIP."
  (let* ((folder (expand-file-name folder))
         (target (expand-file-name target))
         (command
          (if zip
              (format "zip -r %s %s"
                      (shell-quote-argument
                       (expand-file-name
                        (format-time-string "backup-%Y-%m-%d.zip") target))
                      (shell-quote-argument folder))
            (format "rsync -a %s/ %s"
                    (shell-quote-argument folder)
                    (shell-quote-argument target)))))
    (async-shell-command command "*Backup log*")))

;; UI

(defun my/backup ()
  "Open a panel to back up a folder."
  (interactive)
  (switch-to-buffer "*Backup*")
  (kill-all-local-variables)
  (let ((inhibit-read-only t))
    (erase-buffer))
  (remove-overlays)
  (widget-insert "Folder backup\n\n")
  (let* ((folder (widget-create 'editable-field
                                :format "Folder: %v\n"
                                "/home/user/folder"))
         (target (widget-create 'editable-field
                                :format "Target: %v\n"
                                "/home/user/backup"))
         (zip (widget-create 'checkbox nil)))
    (widget-insert " Compress into zip\n\n")
    (widget-create 'push-button
                   :notify (lambda (&rest _) (kill-buffer))
                   "Cancel")
    (widget-insert " ")
    (widget-create 'push-button
                   :notify (lambda (&rest _)
                             (my/backup-run (widget-value folder)
                                            (widget-value target)
                                            (widget-value zip))
                             (kill-buffer))
                   "Run"))
  (use-local-map widget-keymap)
  (widget-setup))

And this scales beyond toys. With this same approach I have built systems of all kinds. For example, this blog's newsletter: I manage subscribers, generate the emails' HTML from org files, send them out, send myself previews and analyze the data. All in Elisp, local, and with a simple control panel integrated with other tools.

The key of the example is that I'm not writing a script for an operating system or an interpreter, but a function for my working environment. I'm building a new workflow, in a space where I can program my own tools, connect them to each other or build one on top of another. I could create an asynchronous notification system to alert me when something finishes, and later connect it to the previous tool at no cost.

And I can do all of this because I have an embedded scripting language, a set of libraries (like widget.el to build graphical interfaces) and a cross-platform runtime that guarantees my scripts will work the same on any operating system. That is a Lispboard.

Programming and development

I want to stress that I separate tasks or environments by tabs. And inside each tab I have a layout with the buffers (or software) I need.

In the IDE tab I usually open:

  • A text editor with some LSP backend and debugger (dap-mode, Eglot, etc.).
  • An AI pair programming partner (chatgpt-shell connected to Claude).
  • A Git client (Magit) to version and review the AI's changes.

Although I keep weaving in temporary ones such as the file manager (Dired, with or without TRAMP), the terminal (vterm), the HTTP client (verb), Docker (docker.el), the web browser (eww) or the project manager (Projectile).

Working in Emacs with this level of integration is a pleasure that's hard to describe. You understand why it makes those who program in it fall in love.

Notes and study

Everything I think, plan or study ends up in plain text. The foundation is org-mode: notes, tasks and planning. On top of it, I manage my study notes with Denote: each note is a file with the metadata in its own name, all linked to each other. And when the study material is a PDF or an EPUB, I read it without leaving, with pdf-tools or nov.

Around them orbit small pieces that complete the routine, like jit-spell underlining my mistakes on the fly (remember the spell checker you configure once and it works everywhere? This is it) and wallabag to read or save articles I want to study later, calmly.

Communication: chats, email and social networks

Although it may not seem like it, over the decades the community has built an excellent pack of communication tools. Even for the same protocol there are several implementations, and each one has its advantages.

In my case, I have a tab exclusively for email with mu4e. It covers every need I can expect from an email client: composing, replying, forwarding, filtering, searching, tagging, marking as read/unread, etc. And it had better do it well, because it's the most important communication channel I have: mailing lists, discussions around a Pull Request, messages from other developers, service notifications, and everything that comes with a personal and professional life.

In another tab I keep a set of chats:

  • IRC with ERC connected to Libera.Chat. Curiously, this is where I've met the most people and been able to talk with many free software developers. I don't understand the eagerness to consider it obsolete.
  • Mastodon with mastodon.el for the Fediverse.
  • Org Social with org-social.el to read the posts on my favorite social network (I'm not impartial, I know, and I don't care).
  • Telegram with telega.el, which lets me read and reply to messages from groups and channels.

I only leave for Teams and Discord, both communication channels at my job. And WhatsApp, whose use in Emacs still doesn't convince me.

News and reading

My main source of information is RSS with Elfeed, almost all personal blogs. I use clients for the aggregators, hackernews-modern and lobsters (both made by me). With Gnus I read some Usenet newsgroups (it's still alive, and it reads wonderfully) and with Elpher I wander around Gemini and Gopher. When I want to open a web link, I give eww, the Emacs web browser, a chance. If it's unreadable, then I go to Firefox or the like (I have a shortcut).

Leisure

The workday is more pleasant with some music playing, hence EMMS backed by mpv. And when I want to disconnect for a while, I like playing chess with chess, either alone or with a friend online. After all, the board is also there to have fun.

The community has an expression for this way of working: living in Emacs. People have been writing about it for decades, and at the end of the article I leave you the readings that have marked me the most. But notice that living in Emacs describes a habit; what I have tried to show you is the consequence. Everything you've seen is not a collection of programs, it's a single board where every module shares a bus with the rest. An interface, a connector, a habitat and a virtual machine (literally: Elisp compiles to bytecode). A Lispboard to play with, or to make your work more efficient.

Its rival is the web browser. It meets more of the requirements than we like to admit: it's a cross-platform virtual machine (for JavaScript) and half of humanity lives inside it, with their email, their chat, their music and their office suite. But it fails at the essential part: tabs are silos that don't share a bus, you can't wire your email to your chat, and no module is yours: each one belongs to a third party that can change it, fill it with ads or shut it down tomorrow. On top of that, access to the host itself is limited: it's not easy to touch the disk or talk to the operating system. The browser is a rented Lispboard, with a landlord. Emacs is the only one you can own.

To wrap up, I want to make clear that using Emacs is not nostalgia: it's that nothing comparable has appeared yet. The 0.1% of us who use it and squeeze it daily do so because it lets us work in a way no other software allows. We will probably have a lifelong relationship with our configuration. There's always something to tweak, improve or automate. And if there isn't, we create it. Emacs is not free because of its GPL license, but because it's the only place where you can live and work your way.

References

  • Why Emacs is still my interface to my computer, the article that encouraged me to write this one.
  • Living in Emacs, by Irreal: living in Emacs as a Japanese garden, a process that seeks perfection but is never complete.
  • Living in the Browser vs. Living in Emacs, also by Irreal, on the dichotomy between the two great interfaces of our time.
  • Emacs: Everything's a Buffer, on the buffer as the universal unit of interaction.
  • How I use Emacs, by Joshua Blais.
  • I Tried Living Completely Using Just Emacs, by FadinGeek: the experiment of living only in Emacs told from the outside.
  • Life in Emacs (revisited), by scut.
  • ¿Por qué los usuarios de Emacs lo usan para todo? (in Spanish). If you want more reasons of my own, it's a good companion piece.

blocks.html_en: I'm an Emacs enthusiast, and maybe that costs this article some credibility. However, I'm not here to tell you how fantastic the GNU editor is, nor to convince you to use it. My goal is to explain why the web browser is its true natural rival, and why Emacs is a Lispboard where you build the way you want to work.

sentences.en: I'm an Emacs enthusiast, and maybe that costs this article some credibility.
sentences.zh: 我是一名 Emacs 爱好者，也许这会降低本文的可信度。

sentences.en: However, I'm not here to tell you how fantastic the GNU editor is, nor to convince you to use it.
sentences.zh: 然而，我并不是要告诉您 GNU 编辑器有多么出色，也不是要说服您使用它。

sentences.en: My goal is to explain why the web browser is its true natural rival, and why Emacs is a Lispboard where you build the way you want to work.
sentences.zh: 我的目标是解释为什么 Web 浏览器是它真正的天然竞争对手，以及为什么 Emacs 是一个 Lispboard，您可以在其中构建自己想要的工作方式。

blocks.html_en: Let's imagine a perfect working environment

sentences.en: Let's imagine a perfect working environment
sentences.zh: 让我们想象一个完美的工作环境

blocks.html_en: To set the stage, let's do a little thought experiment.

sentences.en: To set the stage, let's do a little thought experiment.
sentences.zh: 为了做好准备，让我们做一个小小的思想实验。

blocks.html_en: Suppose I want to work in a single place for all my contexts, both personal and professional. That way I memorize my shortcuts, apply my configurations and never have to switch contexts. Everything lives under the same window.

sentences.en: Suppose I want to work in a single place for all my contexts, both personal and professional.
sentences.zh: 假设我想在一个地方工作以适应我的所有环境，包括个人和专业。

sentences.en: That way I memorize my shortcuts, apply my configurations and never have to switch contexts.
sentences.zh: 这样我就可以记住我的快捷方式，应用我的配置，并且永远不需要切换上下文。

sentences.en: Everything lives under the same window.
sentences.zh: 一切都生活在同一扇窗户下。

blocks.html_en: It's a problem, because it means merging programs that have nothing to do with each other but need to collaborate. For example, a terminal emulator, an email client and a code editor (or IDE). With a layout system, inside the same window, I could have one column with my email inbox, another with the terminal and a third with my IDE ready to write whatever I need. In this space, it would be easy to write a bash script, launch it from the terminal and receive an email with the result. All in the same window, without switching context, with the same shortcuts, the same typography and the same theme. I could even add a fourth column with a Git client to version the script and push it to a remote repository. Or while we're at it, a fifth column with my favorite AI agent, able to read both my code and the rest of the panels.

sentences.en: It's a problem, because it means merging programs that have nothing to do with each other but need to collaborate.
sentences.zh: 这是一个问题，因为这意味着合并彼此无关但需要协作的程序。

sentences.en: For example, a terminal emulator, an email client and a code editor (or IDE).
sentences.zh: 例如，终端仿真器、电子邮件客户端和代码编辑器（或 IDE）。

sentences.en: With a layout system, inside the same window, I could have one column with my email inbox, another with the terminal and a third with my IDE ready to write whatever I need.
sentences.zh: 借助布局系统，在同一个窗口内，我可以将一栏包含我的电子邮件收件箱，另一栏包含终端，第三栏包含我的 IDE，准备好编写我需要的任何内容。

sentences.en: In this space, it would be easy to write a bash script, launch it from the terminal and receive an email with the result.
sentences.zh: 在这个空间中，编写 bash 脚本、从终端启动它并接收包含结果的电子邮件将很容易。

sentences.en: All in the same window, without switching context, with the same shortcuts, the same typography and the same theme.
sentences.zh: 一切都在同一个窗口中，无需切换上下文，具有相同的快捷键、相同的版式和相同的主题。

sentences.en: I could even add a fourth column with a Git client to version the script and push it to a remote repository.
sentences.zh: 我什至可以使用 Git 客户端添加第四列来对脚本进行版本控制并将其推送到远程存储库。

sentences.en: Or while we're at it, a fifth column with my favorite AI agent, able to read both my code and the rest of the panels.
sentences.zh: 或者当我们这样做时，第五列是我最喜欢的人工智能代理，能够读取我的代码和面板的其余部分。

blocks.html_en: We have just created an interface.

sentences.en: We have just created an interface.
sentences.zh: 我们刚刚创建了一个界面。

blocks.html_en: But this wouldn't be the most useful part: the programs would be integrated with each other, each one living in its own scope and, at the same time, in a global one. That means that if I enable the spell checker in the code editor with a certain configuration, it also becomes active in the terminal, in the email client when composing messages, or when I write commits. All with the same interactivity and configuration. They are not four isolated binaries connected with pipes: they are programs written in the same language, running on the same runtime and sharing the same event bus.

sentences.en: But this wouldn't be the most useful part: the programs would be integrated with each other, each one living in its own scope and, at the same time, in a global one.
sentences.zh: 但这并不是最有用的部分：这些程序将相互集成，每个程序都生活在自己的范围内，同时又生活在全球范围内。

sentences.en: That means that if I enable the spell checker in the code editor with a certain configuration, it also becomes active in the terminal, in the email client when composing messages, or when I write commits.
sentences.zh: 这意味着，如果我在具有特定配置的代码编辑器中启用拼写检查器，它也会在撰写消息或编写提交时在终端、电子邮件客户端中变为活动状态。

sentences.en: All with the same interactivity and configuration.
sentences.zh: 所有这些都具有相同的交互性和配置。

sentences.en: They are not four isolated binaries connected with pipes: they are programs written in the same language, running on the same runtime and sharing the same event bus.
sentences.zh: 它们不是通过管道连接的四个独立的二进制文件：它们是用相同语言编写的程序，在相同的运行时上运行并共享相同的事件总线。

blocks.html_en: We have just created a connector.

sentences.en: We have just created a connector.
sentences.zh: 我们刚刚创建了一个连接器。

blocks.html_en: Now let's remove an unnecessary layer: the bash script. If I already have an interpreter embedded in this very window, I can create my own scripts. I don't need an external binary. I no longer need to leave the window, nor touch the operating system. All my scripts, prototypes and workflows can live together.

sentences.en: Now let's remove an unnecessary layer: the bash script.
sentences.zh: 现在让我们删除一个不必要的层：bash 脚本。

sentences.en: If I already have an interpreter embedded in this very window, I can create my own scripts.
sentences.zh: 如果我已经在这个窗口中嵌入了解释器，我就可以创建自己的脚本。

sentences.en: I don't need an external binary.
sentences.zh: 我不需要外部二进制文件。

sentences.en: I no longer need to leave the window, nor touch the operating system.
sentences.zh: 我不再需要离开窗口，也不再触摸操作系统。

sentences.en: All my scripts, prototypes and workflows can live together.
sentences.zh: 我所有的脚本、原型和工作流程都可以共存。

blocks.html_en: We have just created a habitat.

sentences.en: We have just created a habitat.
sentences.zh: 我们刚刚创建了一个栖息地。

blocks.html_en: There's only one problem left with the runtime: tomorrow I'll change laptops, or I'll want to work from a server, and I have no intention of rebuilding my environment from scratch. I want my scripts to work exactly the same regardless of the operating system and the hardware. A script should behave the same way on Windows, on a Raspberry Pi or on a cheap Android device. I need the whole environment to be a cross-platform runtime with bytecode that runs anywhere.

sentences.en: There's only one problem left with the runtime: tomorrow I'll change laptops, or I'll want to work from a server, and I have no intention of rebuilding my environment from scratch.
sentences.zh: 运行时只剩下一个问题：明天我将更换笔记本电脑，或者我想在服务器上工作，而且我无意从头开始重建我的环境。

sentences.en: I want my scripts to work exactly the same regardless of the operating system and the hardware.
sentences.zh: 我希望我的脚本能够以完全相同的方式工作，无论操作系统和硬件如何。

sentences.en: A script should behave the same way on Windows, on a Raspberry Pi or on a cheap Android device.
sentences.zh: 脚本在 Windows、Raspberry Pi 或廉价 Android 设备上的行为方式应相同。

sentences.en: I need the whole environment to be a cross-platform runtime with bytecode that runs anywhere.
sentences.zh: 我需要整个环境成为一个跨平台运行时，其字节码可以在任何地方运行。

blocks.html_en: We have just created a virtual machine.

sentences.en: We have just created a virtual machine.
sentences.zh: 我们刚刚创建了一个虚拟机。

blocks.html_en: Now, to round off the idea, we'll use Lisp as the scripting language and we'll call the whole thing Emacs. Although I prefer to call it something else: a Lispboard. Like an electronics breadboard, a board where every module you plug in shares a bus with all the others, except here the modules are written in Lisp.

sentences.en: Now, to round off the idea, we'll use Lisp as the scripting language and we'll call the whole thing Emacs.
sentences.zh: 现在，为了完善这个想法，我们将使用 Lisp 作为脚本语言，并将整个东西称为 Emacs。

sentences.en: Although I prefer to call it something else: a Lispboard.
sentences.zh: 尽管我更喜欢称之为其他名称：Lispboard。

sentences.en: Like an electronics breadboard, a board where every module you plug in shares a bus with all the others, except here the modules are written in Lisp.
sentences.zh: 就像电子面包板一样，您插入的每个模块都与所有其他模块共享总线，但这里的模块是用 Lisp 编写的。

blocks.html_en: And if during this exercise you thought "this sounds familiar: tabs, applications and a cross-platform virtual machine... that's my browser", hold on to that intuition. We'll come back to it at the end of the article.

sentences.en: And if during this exercise you thought "this sounds familiar: tabs, applications and a cross-platform virtual machine...
sentences.zh: 如果在本练习中您认为“这听起来很熟悉：选项卡、应用程序和跨平台虚拟机......

sentences.en: that's my browser", hold on to that intuition.
sentences.zh: 那是我的浏览器”，坚持这种直觉。

sentences.en: We'll come back to it at the end of the article.
sentences.zh: 我们将在文章的最后再讨论这个问题。

blocks.html_en: What is Emacs not?

sentences.en: What is Emacs not?
sentences.zh: Emacs 不是什么？

blocks.html_en: With everything mentioned so far, I want to debunk some myths surrounding Emacs:

sentences.en: With everything mentioned so far, I want to debunk some myths surrounding Emacs:
sentences.zh: 到目前为止，我想澄清一些关于 Emacs 的误解：

blocks.html_en: • It's not a text editor: it's a complete working environment, with an embedded scripting language, its own software and a cross-platform runtime. The editor is just one more program inside that environment, as are the terminal, the email client, IRC, the file manager or the web browser (yes, Emacs ships with one).

sentences.en: It's not a text editor: it's a complete working environment, with an embedded scripting language, its own software and a cross-platform runtime.
sentences.zh: 它不是一个文本编辑器：它是一个完整的工作环境，具有嵌入式脚本语言、自己的软件和跨平台运行时。

sentences.en: The editor is just one more program inside that environment, as are the terminal, the email client, IRC, the file manager or the web browser (yes, Emacs ships with one).
sentences.zh: 编辑器只是该环境中的又一个程序，终端、电子邮件客户端、IRC、文件管理器或 Web 浏览器也是如此（是的，Emacs 附带了一个程序）。

blocks.html_en: • It's not an operating system: it's a working environment that runs on top of an operating system, and that can replace many of the programs it offers. But it has no drivers, no kernel, no low-level tools. It cannot run without an operating system.

sentences.en: It's not an operating system: it's a working environment that runs on top of an operating system, and that can replace many of the programs it offers.
sentences.zh: 它不是一个操作系统：它是一个在操作系统之上运行的工作环境，并且可以取代它提供的许多程序。

sentences.en: But it has no drivers, no kernel, no low-level tools.
sentences.zh: 但它没有驱动程序、没有内核、没有低级工具。

sentences.en: It cannot run without an operating system.
sentences.zh: 没有操作系统就无法运行。

blocks.html_en: • It's not an IDE: there is no default set of tools focused on programming in a specific language. For that you need LSP backends, debuggers and linters, which are installed as packages. But you can certainly get there; it's one of its most common uses.

sentences.en: It's not an IDE: there is no default set of tools focused on programming in a specific language.
sentences.zh: 它不是 IDE：没有专注于使用特定语言进行编程的默认工具集。

sentences.en: For that you need LSP backends, debuggers and linters, which are installed as packages.
sentences.zh: 为此，您需要 LSP 后端、调试器和 linter，它们作为包安装。

sentences.en: But you can certainly get there; it's one of its most common uses.
sentences.zh: 但你一定能到达那里；这是其最常见的用途之一。

blocks.html_en: What it is: a Lisp interpreter and a framework that run on top of the operating system in the most agnostic way possible, and that reimplement a good part of the programs it offers.

sentences.en: What it is: a Lisp interpreter and a framework that run on top of the operating system in the most agnostic way possible, and that reimplement a good part of the programs it offers.
sentences.zh: 它是什么：一个 Lisp 解释器和一个框架，以尽可能不可知的方式在操作系统之上运行，并重新实现了它提供的程序的很大一部分。

sentences.en: And this scales beyond toys.

blocks.html_en: The key of the example is that I'm not writing a script for an operating system or an interpreter, but a function for my working environment. I'm building a new workflow, in a space where I can program my own tools, connect them to each other or build one on top of another. I could create an asynchronous notification system to alert me when something finishes, and later connect it to the previous tool at no cost.

sentences.en: The key of the example is that I'm not writing a script for an operating system or an interpreter, but a function for my working environment.
sentences.zh: 该示例的关键在于，我不是为操作系统或解释器编写脚本，而是为我的工作环境编写函数。

sentences.en: I'm building a new workflow, in a space where I can program my own tools, connect them to each other or build one on top of another.

sentences.en: I could create an asynchronous notification system to alert me when something finishes, and later connect it to the previous tool at no cost.

sentences.en: That is a Lispboard.

blocks.html_en: The community has an expression for this way of working: living in Emacs. People have been writing about it for decades, and at the end of the article I leave you the readings that have marked me the most. But notice that living in Emacs describes a habit; what I have tried to show you is the consequence. Everything you've seen is not a collection of programs, it's a single board where every module shares a bus with the rest. An interface, a connector, a habitat and a virtual machine (literally: Elisp compiles to bytecode). A Lispboard to play with, or to make your work more efficient.

blocks.html_en: Its rival is the web browser. It meets more of the requirements than we like to admit: it's a cross-platform virtual machine (for JavaScript) and half of humanity lives inside it, with their email, their chat, their music and their office suite. But it fails at the essential part: tabs are silos that don't share a bus, you can't wire your email to your chat, and no module is yours: each one belongs to a third party that can change it, fill it with ads or shut it down tomorrow. On top of that, access to the host itself is limited: it's not easy to touch the disk or talk to the operating system. The browser is a rented Lispboard, with a landlord. Emacs is the only one you can own.

blocks.html_en: To wrap up, I want to make clear that using Emacs is not nostalgia: it's that nothing comparable has appeared yet. The 0.1% of us who use it and squeeze it daily do so because it lets us work in a way no other software allows. We will probably have a lifelong relationship with our configuration. There's always something to tweak, improve or automate. And if there isn't, we create it. Emacs is not free because of its GPL license, but because it's the only place where you can live and work your way.

content_html_zh:
如果 Emacs 的默认缓冲区是终端模拟器，会发生什么？ 从概念上讲，从外部来看，它会被视为一个以 Lisp 作为脚本语言的终端。 也许我们会讨论 iTerm2 与 Emacs，或者 Kitty 与 Emacs，甚至 Bash 与 Emacs。 如果默认缓冲区是 Dired（文件管理器）怎么办？ 历史可能会产生诸如 Total Commander 与 Emacs 或 Finder 与 Emacs 之类的竞争。

但历史却走了另一条路。 我们已经知道，如果您在没有任何配置的情况下启动 Emacs 实例，您将看到一个包含以下消息的缓冲区：

;; This buffer is for text that is not saved, and for Lisp evaluation.
;; To create a file, visit it with ‘C-x C-f’ and enter text in its buffer.

带有嵌入式 Lisp 解释器的记事本。 它天然的竞争对手是带有嵌入式解释器的 Vim（Vimscript）。 然而，让我们都记住的编辑器：q！ 停留在缓冲区中，而 Emacs 不断扩展，包含越来越多的软件，直到它成为操作系统的接口。

我是一名 Emacs 爱好者，也许这会降低本文的可信度。 然而，我并不是要告诉您 GNU 编辑器有多么出色，也不是要说服您使用它。 我的目标是解释为什么 Web 浏览器是它真正的天然竞争对手，以及为什么 Emacs 是一个 Lispboard，您可以在其中构建自己想要的工作方式。

让我们想象一个完美的工作环境

为了做好准备，让我们做一个小小的思想实验。

假设我想在一个地方工作以适应我的所有环境，包括个人和专业。 这样我就可以记住我的快捷方式，应用我的配置，并且永远不需要切换上下文。 一切都生活在同一扇窗户下。

这是一个问题，因为这意味着合并彼此无关但需要协作的程序。 例如，终端仿真器、电子邮件客户端和代码编辑器（或 IDE）。 借助布局系统，在同一个窗口内，我可以将一栏包含我的电子邮件收件箱，另一栏包含终端，第三栏包含我的 IDE，准备好编写我需要的任何内容。 在这个空间中，编写 bash 脚本、从终端启动它并接收包含结果的电子邮件将很容易。 一切都在同一个窗口中，无需切换上下文，具有相同的快捷键、相同的版式和相同的主题。 我什至可以使用 Git 客户端添加第四列来对脚本进行版本控制并将其推送到远程存储库。 或者当我们这样做时，第五列是我最喜欢的人工智能代理，能够读取我的代码和面板的其余部分。

我们刚刚创建了一个界面。

但这并不是最有用的部分：这些程序将相互集成，每个程序都生活在自己的范围内，同时又生活在全球范围内。 这意味着，如果我在具有特定配置的代码编辑器中启用拼写检查器，它也会在撰写消息或编写提交时在终端、电子邮件客户端中变为活动状态。 所有这些都具有相同的交互性和配置。 它们不是通过管道连接的四个独立的二进制文件：它们是用相同语言编写的程序，在相同的运行时上运行并共享相同的事件总线。

我们刚刚创建了一个连接器。

现在让我们删除一个不必要的层：bash 脚本。 如果我已经在这个窗口中嵌入了解释器，我就可以创建自己的脚本。 我不需要外部二进制文件。 我不再需要离开窗口，也不再触摸操作系统。 我所有的脚本、原型和工作流程都可以共存。

我们刚刚创建了一个栖息地。

运行时只剩下一个问题：明天我将更换笔记本电脑，或者我想在服务器上工作，而且我无意从头开始重建我的环境。 我希望我的脚本能够以完全相同的方式工作，无论操作系统和硬件如何。 脚本在 Windows、Raspberry Pi 或廉价 Android 设备上的行为方式应相同。 我需要整个环境成为一个跨平台运行时，其字节码可以在任何地方运行。

我们刚刚创建了一个虚拟机。

现在，为了完善这个想法，我们将使用 Lisp 作为脚本语言，并将整个东西称为 Emacs。 尽管我更喜欢称之为其他名称：Lispboard。 就像电子面包板一样，您插入的每个模块都与所有其他模块共享总线，但这里的模块是用 Lisp 编写的。

如果在本练习中您认为“这听起来很熟悉：选项卡、应用程序和跨平台虚拟机...... 那是我的浏览器”，坚持这种直觉。 我们将在文章的最后再讨论这个问题。

Emacs 不是什么？

到目前为止，我想澄清一些关于 Emacs 的误解：

  • 它不是一个文本编辑器：它是一个完整的工作环境，具有嵌入式脚本语言、自己的软件和跨平台运行时。 编辑器只是该环境中的又一个程序，终端、电子邮件客户端、IRC、文件管理器或 Web 浏览器也是如此（是的，Emacs 附带了一个程序）。
  • 它不是一个操作系统：它是一个在操作系统之上运行的工作环境，并且可以取代它提供的许多程序。 但它没有驱动程序、没有内核、没有低级工具。 没有操作系统就无法运行。
  • 它不是 IDE：没有专注于使用特定语言进行编程的默认工具集。 为此，您需要 LSP 后端、调试器和 linter，它们作为包安装。 但你一定能到达那里；这是其最常见的用途之一。

它是什么：一个 Lisp 解释器和一个框架，以尽可能不可知的方式在操作系统之上运行，并重新实现了它提供的程序的很大一部分。

我的工作流程

脚本编写和原型设计

当您想到：“我要编写一个简单的 bash 脚本来自动执行任务”时，我会想：“我要编写一个 Elisp 函数”。 最重要的是，如果我需要标志或文本输入，我会给它一个图形界面。

例如，假设您需要为自己创建一个脚本，而不是为 CI/CD 进程或 cron 创建脚本。 目标是备份特定文件夹（可能备份到云端）。 你最终可能会得到这样的命令：

./backup.sh /home/user/folder /home/user/backup

但您还可以添加一个用于创建 zip 的标志：

./backup.sh -z /home/user/folder /home/user/backup

好吧，我没有通过终端，而是创建了一个执行相同操作的 Elisp 函数，并向其添加了一个小面板。

I run:

M-x my/backup RET

缓冲区将显示如下界面：

Folder backup

Folder: [ /home/user/folder ]
Target: [ /home/user/backup ]
[ ] Compress into zip

[ Cancel ] [ Run ]

带有输入、复选框和按钮。 无需记住它在哪里，或者它的参数，或者它们的顺序。

如果您以前使用过 Lisp，您会发现代码非常基本：

(require 'widget)

;; Logic

(defun my/backup-run (folder target zip)
  "Sync FOLDER into TARGET with rsync, or compress it to a zip when ZIP."
  (let* ((folder (expand-file-name folder))
         (target (expand-file-name target))
         (command
          (if zip
              (format "zip -r %s %s"
                      (shell-quote-argument
                       (expand-file-name
                        (format-time-string "backup-%Y-%m-%d.zip") target))
                      (shell-quote-argument folder))
            (format "rsync -a %s/ %s"
                    (shell-quote-argument folder)
                    (shell-quote-argument target)))))
    (async-shell-command command "*Backup log*")))

;; UI

(defun my/backup ()
  "Open a panel to back up a folder."
  (interactive)
  (switch-to-buffer "*Backup*")
  (kill-all-local-variables)
  (let ((inhibit-read-only t))
    (erase-buffer))
  (remove-overlays)
  (widget-insert "Folder backup\n\n")
  (let* ((folder (widget-create 'editable-field
                                :format "Folder: %v\n"
                                "/home/user/folder"))
         (target (widget-create 'editable-field
                                :format "Target: %v\n"
                                "/home/user/backup"))
         (zip (widget-create 'checkbox nil)))
    (widget-insert " Compress into zip\n\n")
    (widget-create 'push-button
                   :notify (lambda (&rest _) (kill-buffer))
                   "Cancel")
    (widget-insert " ")
    (widget-create 'push-button
                   :notify (lambda (&rest _)
                             (my/backup-run (widget-value folder)
                                            (widget-value target)
                                            (widget-value zip))
                             (kill-buffer))
                   "Run"))
  (use-local-map widget-keymap)
  (widget-setup))

这超出了玩具的范围。 我用同样的方法构建了各种系统。 例如，此博客的时事通讯：我管理订阅者，从组织文件生成电子邮件的 HTML，将其发送出去，向自己发送预览并分析数据。 一切都在本地 Elisp 中，并通过与其他工具集成的简单控制面板进行。

该示例的关键在于，我不是为操作系统或解释器编写脚本，而是为我的工作环境编写函数。 我正在构建一个新的工作流程，在这个空间中我可以对自己的工具进行编程，将它们相互连接或在另一个工具之上构建一个工具。 我可以创建一个异步通知系统，在某件事完成时提醒我，然后免费将其连接到以前的工具。

我可以做到这一切，因为我有一个嵌入式脚本语言、一组库（例如用于构建图形界面的 widget.el）和一个跨平台运行时，可以保证我的脚本在任何操作系统上都可以正常工作。 那是一个 Lispboard。

编程与开发

我想强调的是，我通过选项卡分隔任务或环境。 在每个选项卡内，我都有一个布局，其中包含我需要的缓冲区（或软件）。

在 IDE 选项卡中我通常打开：

  • 带有一些 LSP 后端和调试器（dap 模式、Eglot 等）的文本编辑器。
  • 一个 AI 结对编程伙伴（chatgpt-shell 连接到 Claude）。
  • Git 客户端 (Magit) 用于版本控制和审查 AI 的更改。

尽管我一直在编织临时的东西，例如文件管理器（Dired，有或没有 TRAMP）、终端（vterm）、HTTP 客户端（verb）、Docker（docker.el）、Web 浏览器（eww）或项目管理器（Projectile）。

在具有这种集成级别的 Emacs 中工作是一种难以形容的乐趣。 你明白为什么它会让那些在其中编程的人坠入爱河。

笔记与学习

我的所有想法、计划或研究都以纯文本形式结束。 基础是组织模式：笔记、任务和计划。 最重要的是，我用 Denote 管理我的学习笔记：每个笔记都是一个文件，其元数据以其自己的名称命名，所有笔记都相互链接。 当学习材料是 PDF 或 EPUB 时，我会用 pdf-tools 或 nov 不离开而阅读它。

在它们周围运行一些小片段来完成例程，比如 jit-spell 强调我在运行中所犯的错误（还记得您配置一次的拼写检查器，它在任何地方都可以工作吗？ 就是这样）和wallabag来阅读或保存我想稍后学习的文章，平静地。

沟通：聊天、电子邮件和社交网络

尽管看起来可能并非如此，但几十年来，社区已经构建了一套出色的通信工具。 即使同一个协议也有多种实现方式，每一种都有其优点。

就我而言，我有一个专门用于使用 mu4e 发送电子邮件的选项卡。 它涵盖了我对电子邮件客户端的所有需求：撰写、回复、转发、过滤、搜索、标记、标记为已读/未读等。它最好做得很好，因为它是我拥有的最重要的沟通渠道：邮件列表、围绕拉取请求的讨论、来自其他开发人员的消息、服务通知以及个人和职业生活中的一切。

在另一个选项卡中，我保留了一组聊天记录：

  • IRC 和 ERC 连接到 Libera.Chat。 奇怪的是，这是我遇到最多人并能够与许多自由软件开发人员交谈的地方。 我不明白为什么急于认为它已经过时了。
  • 乳齿象与乳齿象.el 的 Fediverse。
  • Org Social 与 org-social.el 阅读我最喜欢的社交网络上的帖子（我不公正，我知道，而且我不在乎）。
  • Telegram 与 telega.el，让我可以阅读和回复来自群组和频道的消息。

我只离开 Teams 和 Discord，这两个渠道都是我工作中的沟通渠道。 还有 WhatsApp，它在 Emacs 中的使用仍然不能让我信服。

新闻与阅读

我的信息主要来源是RSS和Elfeed，几乎都是个人博客。 我使用聚合器、hackernews-modern 和 lobster 的客户端（都是我制作的）。 我用 Gnus 阅读了一些 Usenet 新闻组（它仍然存在，而且读起来很棒），用 Elpher 我在 Gemini 和 Gopher 中闲逛。 当我想打开一个 Web 链接时，我给 eww（Emacs Web 浏览器）一个机会。 如果它不可读，那么我会使用 Firefox 等（我有一个快捷方式）。

闲暇

播放一些音乐会让工作日变得更加愉快，因此 EMMS 有 mpv 支持。 当我想暂时断开连接时，我喜欢下棋下棋，无论是独自一人还是与网上的朋友一起下棋。 毕竟，董事会也是为了玩得开心。

社区对这种工作方式有一个说法：生活在 Emacs 中。 几十年来人们一直在写关于它的文章，在文章的最后，我给你们留下了对我影响最大的读物。 但请注意，使用 Emacs 描述的是一种习惯；我试图向你们展示的是结果。 您所看到的所有东西都不是程序的集合，而是一个单板，其中每个模块都与其他模块共享总线。 一个接口、一个连接器、一个栖息地和一个虚拟机（字面意思：Elisp 编译为字节码）。 一个可以玩的 Lispboard，或者让你的工作更有效率。

它的竞争对手是网络浏览器。 它满足的要求比我们愿意承认的还要多：它是一个跨平台虚拟机（用于 JavaScript），一半的人类生活在其中，包括他们的电子邮件、聊天、音乐和办公套件。 但它在关键部分失败了：选项卡是不共享总线的孤岛，你无法将电子邮件连接到聊天室，并且没有模块是你的：每个模块都属于第三方，可以更改它，在其中填充广告或明天将其关闭。 最重要的是，对主机本身的访问受到限制：触摸磁盘或与操作系统对话并不容易。 浏览器是租用的Lispboard，跟房东有关系。 Emacs 是您唯一可以拥有的一款。

最后，我想澄清的是，使用 Emacs 并不是怀旧：而是还没有出现任何类似的东西。 我们中的 0.1% 每天使用它并使用它，因为它让我们能够以其他软件不允许的方式工作。 我们可能会与我们的配置建立终生的关系。 总有一些东西需要调整、改进或自动化。 如果没有，我们就创建它。 Emacs 之所以免费并不是因为它有 GPL 许可证，而是因为它是您可以按照自己的方式生活和工作的唯一地方。
